Field Notes From the River Restoration Corridor

By Theo Martin | March 2, 2026

Restoration crews spent the winter reopening side channels that had been cut off by decades of concrete reinforcement. As spring runoff began, volunteers reported clearer water in backwaters where young salmon rest before moving downstream.

Monitoring teams now sample temperature and dissolved oxygen every six hours. Early data shows narrower temperature swings in restored bends, suggesting that deeper channels and new shade from native willow plantings are reducing summer stress for juvenile fish.

Residents living near the corridor said they notice more birds and fewer flooding concerns during moderate storms. Project scientists caution that major ecological shifts take years, but they say first-season trends support continued investment in habitat-first design.

A fourth survey in late summer will compare insect abundance against pre-project baselines. Those counts are expected to influence next year's planting plans and determine where crews should prioritize additional gravel beds for spawning.
